sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Look at the bottom region of the board more carefully
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Bottom 10 rows:")
for i in range(54, min(64, len(ascii_lines))):
    print(f"Row {i}: {ascii_lines[i]}")

# Also check segmentation for bottom objects
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nAll objects with row > 50:")
for n in nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
    if min_r > 50:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={b[:4]}...")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

So in level 1, the answer was simply to match the reference order in a single frame.
In level 2, there are two frames. The question is how the reference maps to the two frames.
What if the split is based on the L-shape position? The L-shape is in the 3rd position of the upper frame. So:
- Upper frame positions: 1, 2, [L-shape], 4

If the L-shape represents position 3, then:
